sections.h: What you must be able to do
points: Solve quadratics by factorising, by the formula $x=\frac{-b\pm\sqrt{b^2-4ac}}{2a}$ (to 2 decimal places when asked), and by completing the square (answers in surd form).
Solve quadratic inequalities and give the answer in interval or inequality notation.
Solve simultaneous equations where one is linear and one is quadratic (or contains $xy$ or fractions).
Solve exponential equations (common factor, or quadratic in $2^x$) and surd equations (isolate, square, CHECK).
Use the discriminant $\Delta=b^2-4ac$ to reason about the nature of roots and find values of $k$.

sections.h: Core methods
points: Always rearrange to $=0$ (or $>0/<0$) before factorising or using the formula.
Inequalities: find the roots, sketch the parabola, read off where it is above/below the axis. Never divide by a variable expression.
Simultaneous: make one variable the subject of the LINEAR equation, substitute into the quadratic, solve, then back-substitute for both pairs.
Exponential equations like $2^{2x}-2^{x+2}-32=0$: substitute $k=2^x$ to get a quadratic in $k$; reject $k\le0$.
Surd equations: after squaring, test EVERY answer in the original equation — squaring creates extraneous roots.
Nature of roots: real $\Leftrightarrow\Delta\ge0$; equal $\Leftrightarrow\Delta=0$; non-real $\Leftrightarrow\Delta<0$; rational $\Leftrightarrow\Delta$ a perfect square.

sections.h: Common traps
points: Rounding too early in the formula — keep 4 decimals, round at the end.
Forgetting the negative root when solving $x^2=c$ (answer: $x=\pm\sqrt{c}$).
Inequality answers written as a single interval when the parabola opens up and the solution is OUTSIDE the roots ($x<a$ or $x>b$).
Not checking surd-equation answers — usually exactly one is valid.
In $k$-questions, solving $\Delta=0$ when the question asked for real UNEQUAL roots ($\Delta>0$).

sections.h: How the exam asks it (Q1, ±25 marks)
points: Q1.1 is a ladder: factorise (2-3), formula to 2 d.p. (3-4), inequality (3-4), then an exponential and/or surd equation (4-5 each).
Q1.2 is the simultaneous pair for 5-6 marks.

The last part is a level-4 twist for 3-4 marks: telescoping products, integer exponent equations like $2^{m+1}+2^m=3^{n+2}-3^n$, or reasoning about all possible values. Factor out common powers and write things out — do not skip it, part marks are real.

sections.h: What you must be able to do
points: Arithmetic: $T_n=a+(n-1)d$, $S_n=\frac{n}{2}[2a+(n-1)d]$ — find terms, positions and sums.
Geometric: $T_n=ar^{n-1}$, $S_n=\frac{a(r^n-1)}{r-1}$, $S_\infty=\frac{a}{1-r}$ for $-1<r<1$.
Quadratic patterns: $T_n=an^2+bn+c$ where the SECOND difference $=2a$.
Sigma notation: evaluate $\sum$ expressions and write given sums in sigma notation.
Mixed problems where consecutive expressions in $x$ form an AP or GP.

sections.h: Core methods
points: Number of terms from $k=m$ to $n$ is $n-m+1$ — the classic sigma slip.
Quadratic pattern: first differences form an AP. Questions like 'given $T_{99}$, find $T_{98}$' only need the 98th first difference: $T_{99}-T_{98}=d_{98}$.
To find which term equals a value in a GP, solve $ar^{n-1}=\text{value}$ using prime factorising or logs.
Convergence questions: set up $-1<r<1$ where $r$ is an expression in $x$, and solve the double inequality.
AP condition: $T_2-T_1=T_3-T_2$. GP condition: $\frac{T_2}{T_1}=\frac{T_3}{T_2}$. Always check answers back in the sequence.

sections.h: Common traps
points: Using $n$ where the question wants the TERM and vice versa — answer the actual question.
Sum vs term: $S_n$ questions answered with $T_n$ formulas.
Forgetting $r\neq0$ and rejecting values of $x$ that make consecutive terms zero.
In context problems (areas, lengths), forgetting units or squaring the ratio for areas.

sections.h: How the exam asks it (Q2–Q3, ±25 marks)
points: One routine AP/sigma question, one quadratic-pattern question with a clever twist, and one GP question often in a real context (e.g. circles shrinking by a factor — find the radius pattern, sum of areas, which circle has a given size).
Sum-to-infinity appears most years, either 'calculate $S_\infty$' or 'for which $x$ does the series converge'.

sections.h: What you must be able to do
points: Parabola $y=a(x+p)^2+q$ / $y=ax^2+bx+c$: intercepts, turning point $x=-\frac{b}{2a}$, axis of symmetry, sketch.
Hyperbola $y=\frac{a}{x+p}+q$: asymptotes $x=-p$, $y=q$, intercepts, axes of symmetry $y=\pm(x+p)+q$.
Exponential $y=a\cdot b^{x+p}+q$ and its inverse $y=\log_b x$; sketch both and state domain/range.
Inverses: swap $x$ and $y$, solve for $y$; restrict a parabola's domain so $f^{-1}$ is a function.
Read solutions off graphs: $f(x)\ge0$, $f(x)>g(x)$, $f(x)\cdot g(x)\le0$, lengths between graphs, average gradient.

sections.h: Core methods
points: Finding equations from a sketch: substitute the clearly-labelled points (intercepts, turning point, asymptote info) into the right general form.
Vertical distance between $f$ and $g$ at $x$: $|f(x)-g(x)|$. MAXIMUM vertical distance on an interval: maximise $f(x)-g(x)$ (complete the square or differentiate).
Inverse of exponential: $y=a^x\Rightarrow f^{-1}: y=\log_a x$. A point $(m;n)$ on $f$ reflects to $(n;m)$.
Tangency of a line to a parabola: set them equal and force $\Delta=0$.
Transformations: $f(x)+q$ shifts up, $f(x+p)$ shifts left, $f(-x)$ reflects in the $y$-axis, $-f(x)$ in the $x$-axis — track what happens to asymptotes and turning points.

sections.h: Common traps
points: Domain/range written with the wrong variable or bracket type — range of $y=a^x-4$ is $y>-4$, not $y\ge-4$.
Asymptote signs: $y=\frac{a}{x+p}+q$ has vertical asymptote at $x=-p$ (not $+p$).
For $f(x)\ge0$ on a hyperbola, remember the piece beyond the vertical asymptote.
Inverse notation: $f^{-1}(x)$ is NOT $\frac{1}{f(x)}$.

sections.h: How the exam asks it (Q4–Q6, ±35 marks)
points: Three questions: an exponential(+inverse/log) one, a hyperbola one, and a parabola-with-line one.

Fixtures: 'write down the asymptote/range' (1-2 free marks), 'determine the equation of...' (3-4), 'for which values of $x$ is...' (2-3), and a final level-4 part: maximum vertical distance or a tangent/shift with an unknown $m$ solved via the discriminant.
2024 example: given $k(x)=f(x+m)$, find $m$ so a line is tangent to $k$ — substitute, set equal, $\Delta=0$, solve for $m$.

sections.h: What you must be able to do
points: Compound growth/decay: $A=P(1\pm i)^n$; straight-line: $A=P(1\pm ni)$.
Convert nominal to effective: $1+i_{eff}=(1+\frac{i_{nom}}{m})^m$.
Future value of an annuity $F=\frac{x[(1+i)^n-1]}{i}$ (savings, sinking funds).
Present value $P=\frac{x[1-(1+i)^{-n}]}{i}$ (loans, 'how many withdrawals').
Solve for $n$ with logarithms; find outstanding balances; handle timeline changes (extra payments, gaps, rate changes).

sections.h: Core methods
points: Match $i$ and $n$ to the compounding period FIRST (monthly rate = nominal ÷ 12; months not years).
Draw a timeline for anything beyond one step. Mark deposits, withdrawals, rate changes and 'now'.
Outstanding balance after $k$ payments = PV of the remaining $n-k$ payments (fastest), or grow the loan $k$ months and subtract the grown payments.
'How many months earlier / how many withdrawals': set up the annuity equation with $n$ unknown, isolate $(1+i)^n$ (or $(1+i)^{-n}$), take logs.
Total interest = (all money paid) − (loan amount).

sections.h: Common traps
points: Using years for $n$ with a monthly rate (or vice versa).
Annuity term counts: first payment 'one month from now' vs 'immediately' changes $n$ or adds a factor $(1+i)$.
Depreciation: reducing balance uses $(1-i)^n$; straight line SUBTRACTS the same amount each year and can reach zero.
Rounding money before the final answer — keep full precision, round to cents at the end.

sections.h: How the exam asks it (Q7-ish, ±15 marks)
points: A short compound/decay part (2-4), often a nominal/effective or rate/time solve (2-3), then a loan or annuity main course (6-9).

The final part is a level-4 timeline twist: lump-sum paid mid-loan → how many months earlier does it end (2024, 7 marks); jackpot invested → how many R20 000 withdrawals possible (2023, 5 marks). These always end in a log solve — practise that move until it's automatic.

sections.h: What you must be able to do
points: First principles: $f'(x)=\lim_{h\to0}\frac{f(x+h)-f(x)}{h}$ for $ax^2+bx+c$, $ax^3$, $\frac{a}{x}$.
Differentiate after rewriting: surds to powers ($\sqrt[3]{x^7}=x^{7/3}$), fractions to negative powers, expand products.
Tangents: gradient $=f'(a)$, then a line through $(a;f(a))$.
Cubic graphs: intercepts, turning points ($f'(x)=0$), point of inflection, sketch and interpret.
Optimisation and rates of change in context (area, volume, motion).

sections.h: Core methods
points: Write the first-principles limit definition every time — the structure earns marks even if algebra slips.
Turning points: solve $f'(x)=0$; concavity via $f''$ ($f''>0$ concave up); inflection at $f''(x)=0$.
For 'values of $k$ so $f(x)+k$ has three (positive) roots': slide the graph vertically and trap the $x$-axis strictly between the turning-point $y$-values.
Given the graph of $f'$ (a parabola): $f$ increases where $f'>0$; $f$ has turning points at the ZEROS of $f'$; $f$ has its inflection at the turning point of $f'$.
Optimisation recipe: write the constraint, express the target in ONE variable, differentiate, set $=0$, check it's a max/min, answer the actual question.

sections.h: Common traps
points: Differentiating $\frac{2}{x^2}$ as if it were $2x^2$ — rewrite as $2x^{-2}$ first ($\Rightarrow-4x^{-3}$).
Notation marks: $\frac{dy}{dx}$, $f'(x)$, $D_x$ — answer in the notation asked.
Forgetting to give BOTH coordinates of turning points when sketching.
In motion problems, mixing up $s$ (distance), $s'$ (speed) and $s''$ (acceleration).

sections.h: How the exam asks it (Q8–Q10, ±35 marks)
points: Q8-type: first principles (5) + two rewrite-and-differentiate parts (2-4 each) + a tangent or inverse-link part.
Q9-type: a cubic — either sketch it fully, or interpret a given sketch (decreasing interval, concavity, $k$-shift root counting, $x$-intercepts of $f'$).
Q10-type: optimisation/rates in context worth 6-8 (fence against a wall, box volume, cyclist speed $s'(t)=-3t^2+18t$ → max speed and distance).
The $f'$-graph interpretation question is the most commonly botched — drill the translation table between $f$ and $f'$.

sections.h: What you must be able to do
points: Rules: $P(A\text{ or }B)=P(A)+P(B)-P(A\text{ and }B)$; complementary $P(\text{not }A)=1-P(A)$.
Independent events: $P(A\text{ and }B)=P(A)\cdot P(B)$; mutually exclusive: $P(A\text{ and }B)=0$ — and TEST for these with numbers.
Venn diagrams (2 or 3 sets) with an unknown region $x$; tree diagrams for two-stage events.
Counting: fundamental counting principle, arrangements with restrictions, repeated letters (divide by $k!$), and probability = favourable/total arrangements.

sections.h: Core methods
points: Venn: fill from the innermost overlap outward; sum all regions to the total to solve for $x$.
'At least one' $=1-P(\text{none})$ — almost always the fastest route.
Independence test: compute $P(A\text{ and }B)$ and $P(A)\cdot P(B)$ separately and compare — show both numbers.
Restrictions first: place the constrained position/letter, then multiply the remaining choices.
Letter arrangements with repeats: $\frac{n!}{k_1!\,k_2!\cdots}$.
Arrangement probability: count the favourable line-ups (positions for the special pair × their internal order × arrangements of the rest) over $n!$.

sections.h: Common traps
points: Confusing mutually exclusive with independent — they are almost opposites.
Tree diagrams where second-stage probabilities don't change when they should (or branches don't sum to 1).
Counting codes: 'may not be repeated' changes each slot's count (26, then 25...).
Forgetting that 'divisible by 5 / odd digit' pins the LAST slot — fill it first.

sections.h: How the exam asks it (Q11–Q12, ±15 marks)
points: One question on rules + a Venn or tree (2024: draw the Venn for three exam subjects, P(at least two), test independence with calculations — 9 marks).

One counting question: codes/plates/photos with escalating restrictions, ending with a 2-4 mark twist like a percentage increase or an arrangement probability ('probability that exactly 5 learners stand between the two youngest').
Show the multiplication line (e.g. $26\times10\times25\times5$) — method marks live there.

sections.h: What you must be able to do
points: Mean, median, mode, range, IQR, five-number summary, box-and-whisker plots and skewness.
Population standard deviation $\sigma$ (calculator) and 'within one standard deviation' counts.
Ogives: read median/quartiles, counts above/below a value, identify the modal class.
Least-squares regression $\hat{y}=a+bx$ and correlation $r$ by calculator; predict and interpret.
Effects on mean/median/σ of adding a constant, scaling, or an outlier; the $1{,}5\times$IQR outlier test.

sections.h: Core methods
points: Five-number summary: order the data first; for $n$ values the median is at position $\frac{n+1}{2}$.
Ogive reading: go to $\frac{n}{2}$ (or $\frac{n}{4}$, $\frac{3n}{4}$) on the cumulative axis, across to the curve, down to the value.
Regression by calculator: two-variable STAT mode, record $a$, $b$, $r$ to at least two decimals, keep them stored for predictions.
Interpolation (inside the data range) is trustworthy; extrapolation (outside) is not — say so when asked.
Adding a constant $c$ to every value: mean/median shift by $c$, $\sigma$ UNCHANGED. Multiplying by $c$: everything scales.

sections.h: Common traps
points: Using the sample ($n-1$) standard deviation — NSC uses population $\sigma_n$.
Skewness direction: right/positive skew has the LONG tail to the right (mean > median).
Predicting with rounded $a$, $b$ then over-rounding the answer — state the rounding you used.
Answering 'how does σ change' with a number when only the direction/no-change is asked.

sections.h: How the exam asks it (Q1–Q2, ±20 marks)
points: One raw-data/summary question (five-number summary, box plot, σ, outlier test) and one grouped/ogive or regression question.
2024: regression on rugby players (find $\hat{y}$, $r$, predict, mean, effect of +5 on σ, max increase to meet a target) + an ogive with a work-from-home rule interpretation.
Expect one 'interpret in context' sentence answer — write a short, specific sentence, not an essay.

sections.h: What you must be able to do
points: Distance, midpoint, gradient; equations of lines $y=mx+c$ / $y-y_1=m(x-x_1)$.
Parallel ($m_1=m_2$) and perpendicular ($m_1m_2=-1$) conditions; collinearity.
Inclination: $\tan\theta=m$, with $\theta\in[0°;180°)$; angles between lines from inclinations.
Circles $(x-a)^2+(y-b)^2=r^2$: find centre/radius by completing the square; tangents (⊥ radius) at a point.
Properties of triangles and quadrilaterals on the plane; ratios and areas.

sections.h: Core methods
points: Negative gradient → obtuse inclination: $\theta=180°-\tan^{-1}|m|$.
'Show that the equation of the circle is...': complete the square in full — every balancing step visible.
Tangent at $P$: gradient of radius $MP$ first, then $m_{tan}=-\frac{1}{m_{MP}}$, then the line through $P$.
Ratio of segments on a line: use the x- (or y-) differences — for B on DC, $\frac{DB}{DC}=\frac{x_B-x_D}{x_C-x_D}$.
Area ratios with a parallel line: the triangles are similar, so areas scale by the SQUARE of the side ratio.
Unknown point from gradient + length: two equations (gradient gives a linear relation, distance a quadratic) — solve and pick the quadrant that matches the diagram.

sections.h: Common traps
points: Radius vs diameter mix-ups, and $r^2$ left as $r$.
Perpendicular gradient sign errors — flip AND negate.
Assuming a diagram property (like a right angle) that must be PROVEN.
Chained questions: check each early answer (gradient, then equation, then length) because later parts reuse them.

sections.h: How the exam asks it (Q3–Q4, ±40 marks)
points: Q3: a triangle/quad vertex chain — gradient → equation → show a coordinate → length → ratio → area ratio → find the unknown vertex from gradient + length (2024's 6-mark finale).
Q4: THE circle question — tangent equation, show the circle's equation, a 'show that $d=...$' point-on-circle part, an angle via inclinations, and a perpendicularity/tangent proof to finish.
Both questions print the diagram — redraw key parts and mark known values as you go.

sections.h: What you must be able to do
points: Exact values from a point $P(x;y)$ on the terminal arm, and from given ratios with quadrant restrictions — no calculator.
Reduction formulae ($180°\pm x$, $360°\pm x$, $-x$, co-functions $90°\pm x$) and special angles.
Compound and double angles: $\sin(\alpha\pm\beta)$, $\cos(\alpha\pm\beta)$, $\sin2\alpha$, all three forms of $\cos2\alpha$.
Prove identities and state where they're undefined; solve general solutions and interval equations.
Sketch/read $\sin$, $\cos$, $\tan$ graphs with amplitude/period/shifts; solve equations FROM graphs.
Sine rule, cosine rule, area rule in 2D and 3D problems.

sections.h: Core methods
points: Point on terminal arm: compute $r=\sqrt{x^2+y^2}$, then read ratios with correct signs (CAST diagram).
$\cos2A$ from $\cos A$: use $2\cos^2A-1$; from $\sin A$: use $1-2\sin^2A$ — pick the form that matches what you have.
Identities: factorise using the $\cos2A$ form that pairs with its neighbours (e.g. $\cos2A+\cos A+1=2\cos^2A+\cos A$).

General solutions: $\sin x=\sin\alpha\Rightarrow x=\alpha+k360°$ or $x=180°-\alpha+k360°$; $\cos x=\cos\alpha\Rightarrow x=\pm\alpha+k360°$; $\tan x=\tan\alpha\Rightarrow x=\alpha+k180°$, $k\in\mathbb{Z}$.
Co-function equations like $\sin(x+20°)=\cos2x$: rewrite $\cos2x=\sin(90°-2x)$ and equate both families.
3D problems: work triangle by triangle; the shared side bridges them. Right angles come from 'vertical' meets 'horizontal'.
Max/min: rewrite in terms of a single $\sin/\cos$ (range $[-1;1]$) and reason — no calculus.

sections.h: Common traps
points: Dropping the second solution family in general solutions.
Sign errors in quadrants II–IV when working without a calculator — draw the sketch triangle every time.
Dividing an equation by $\cos x$ (losing solutions) instead of factorising.
Forgetting $k\in\mathbb{Z}$ (loses a mark) or leaving answers outside a stated interval.
Using degrees-mode maths on a calculator set to radians.

sections.h: How the exam asks it (Q5–Q8, ±50 marks)
points: Q5: no-calculator values from a point + a compound-angle evaluation + an 'express in terms of $p$' part.
Q6: derive a compound identity, prove a reduction-heavy identity (6 marks), solve an equation in an interval, max/min of a trig expression.
Q7: graphs — asymptotes, periods, draw one graph, then 'use the graphs to determine the general solution' of a combined equation (spot that it means: where the drawn graphs intersect).
Q8: the 2D/3D problem (±9-13 marks): building/mast with angles of elevation across 2-3 triangles — sine rule for the bridge side, cosine/area rule to finish.

sections.h: What you must be able to do
points: All circle theorems with their reason names: line from centre ⊥ chord; angle at centre $=2\times$ angle at circumference; angles in same segment; angle in semi-circle; cyclic quad (opposite angles supplementary, exterior = interior opposite); tangents from a point; tan-chord.
Proportionality: a line parallel to one side of a triangle divides the other two proportionally (and its converse); the midpoint theorem.
Similarity: equiangular triangles ⇒ sides in proportion, building to product-of-sides results.
PROVE the examinable theorems from scratch — they are asked for real marks (2024: proportionality theorem, 6 marks).

sections.h: Core methods
points: Statement (reason) format ALWAYS — e.g. $\hat{B}_1=\hat{D}$ (tan-chord). No reason, no mark.
Angle chase: transfer the given angles onto the diagram, then hunt tangent pairs, diameters, cyclic quads and isosceles radii triangles.
To prove sides equal: find equal angles in the same triangle (isosceles) or congruent/similar triangles.
To prove a product like $AG\cdot AD=AC\cdot AF$: find the two similar triangles whose ratio statement cross-multiplies into it — write vertices in corresponding order.
Composite finals like $GF^2=\frac{BC\cdot FC\cdot AF}{AD}$: multiply/divide the results of the previous parts — the exam builds them in sequence.
Ratios with the centre: perpendicular from centre bisects the chord; combine with the midpoint theorem for 1:2 relationships.

sections.h: Common traps
points: Wrong or vague reasons ('angles in circle') — learn the exact accepted phrasing list.
Using what you must still prove (circular logic) — especially in 'hence' questions.
Similar triangles written in non-corresponding order, wrecking the ratio.
Assuming a line is a diameter or tangent when it isn't given.
Skipping the theorem proofs when studying — they are the most predictable 5-6 marks in the paper.

sections.h: How the exam asks it (Q9–Q11, ±40 marks)
points: Q9: a one-circle rider — calculate an angle with reasons, then 'hence prove' equal chords / cyclic quad (±6 marks).
Q10: a theorem PROOF (bookwork) + a proportionality/midpoint application with a numeric length to finish (±15 marks).
Q11: the grand rider (±20 marks): often two circles with a common tangent — find 4 angles equal to $x$ (6), prove a side-product (4), prove similarity (4), then the composite product finale (6).
The instruction 'Provide reasons for your statements' is printed above these questions — treat every line without a reason as a lost mark.
